question: [File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getTokensRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user flood GET /tokens with the maximum allowed `limit` value combined with a wildcard `name` ILIKE filter (e.g., `name=%`) to trigger full-table sequential scans on the token and entity tables, exhausting DB CPU and connection pool slots so no new transaction confirmations can be processed?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: extractSqlFromTokenRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user submit repeated GET /tokens requests with many repeated `token.id=eq:X` values to grow the `inClause` array unboundedly, causing `params.push(inClause)` to pass a massive array to PostgreSQL's `= any($N)` operator, resulting in query plan explosion and DB thread exhaustion that prevents transaction confirmation?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getTokenInfoRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user send thousands of concurrent GET /tokens/:tokenId requests with a valid `timestamp=lte:X` filter to force `buildHistoryQuery()` to execute expensive UNION ALL queries across both `token`, `token_history`, `entity`, `entity_history`, `custom_fee`, and `custom_fee_history` tables simultaneously, saturating the DB connection pool and halting transaction processing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: buildHistoryQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a timestamp filter that causes `buildHistoryQuery()` to generate a UNION ALL subquery with `order by modified_timestamp desc limit 1` on both the live and history tables, and by sending this concurrently at high rate, exhaust PostgreSQL worker processes so that the ingestion pipeline cannot write new consensus transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: extractSqlFromTokenBalancesRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user send GET /tokens/:tokenId/balances with a `timestamp=eq:X` filter to trigger `balances.getAccountBalanceTimestampRange()` as an additional async DB call before the main query, doubling DB connection consumption per request, and by flooding this endpoint exhaust the connection pool causing total service unavailability?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getNftTransferHistoryRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user send GET /tokens/:tokenId/nfts/:serialNumber/transactions with no timestamp filter to trigger a two-phase DB query (first `extractSqlFromNftTransferHistoryRequest()` then `nftTransactionHistoryDetailsQuery`), consuming two DB connections per request, and by flooding this endpoint at scale exhaust the pool and prevent transaction confirmation queries?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: extractSqlFromNftTransferHistoryRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user send GET /tokens/:tokenId/nfts/:serialNumber/transactions with `limit=<max>` to force the UNION ALL query across `nft`, `nft_history`, and `entity` tables with a large limit, causing expensive multi-table scans that hold DB connections open for extended periods and starve the transaction ingestion pipeline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getNftTransferHistoryRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger the `jsonb_path_query_array()` call inside `nftTransactionHistoryDetailsQuery` with a large `timestamps` array (via `rows.map((r) => r.timestamp)`) by choosing a token/serial with many history entries, causing a CPU-intensive JSONB path evaluation on the DB server that blocks other queries including transaction writes?
